2:
### Market Square Historic District (1972 - 1999)
---  

 A delisted national historic district that encompassed 19 contributing buildings in the central business district of St. Joseph. <!--more--> It developed between about 1850 and 1940, and included representative examples of Renaissance Revival style architecture. The primary building is the Bank of the State of Missouri (1859). 

 A victim of the urban renewal movement, most of the remaining buildings were demolished in 1973. 

The six remaining buildings in the district were subsumed under the Missouri Valley Trust Company Historic District. 

Source: Wikipedia
3:
### Missouri Valley Trust Company Historic District (1975)
---  

Formerly known as the Market Square Historic District, this national historic district encompasses six contributing buildings in the central business district of St. Joseph. <!--more-->It developed between about 1859 and the 1860s, and includes representative examples of Renaissance Revival style architecture.  The primary building is the Bank of the State of Missouri (1859).

The district was subsumed under the St. Joseph's Commerce and Banking Historic District.
5: ### Robidoux Hill Historic District (1989) --- A national historic district that encompasses 61 contributing buildings in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. It developed between about 1865 and 1909, and includes representative examples of Italianate, Second Empire, and Queen Anne style architecture. Located in the district is the separately listed Edmond Jacques Eckel House designed by architect Edmond Jacques Eckel (1845–1934). Other notable buildings include the Lemon House (1871), Donovan House (c. 1865, 1895), McKinney House (1887), Inslee House (c. 1867), Jonathan M. Bassett (c. 1860, 1880s), and U.S. Weather Bureau Building (1909). Source: Wikipedia More Info
6:
### Central-North Commercial Historic District (1991)
--- 

A national historic district that encompasses 18 contributing buildings in an industrial/commercial section of St. Joseph west of the central business district. <!--more--> It developed between about 1885 and 1929, and includes representative examples of Italianate and Classical Revival style architecture. 

Located in the district is the separately listed John D. Richardson Dry Goods Company. Other notable buildings include a number of warehouse/light manufacturing facilities some of which were designed by the architectural firm of Eckel & Aldrich. 

Source: Wikipedia
7:
### Museum Hill Historic District (1991*)
---  

A national historic district that encompasses 248 contributing buildings in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. <!--more--> It developed between about 1860 and 1942, and includes representative examples of Italianate, Second Empire, Queen Anne, Tudor Revival, American Foursquare, and Bungalow/American Craftsman style architecture. 

Located in the district is the separately listed Robidoux School. Other notable buildings include the First Congregational Church (1890), Francis Street Methodist Church (1905), First Baptist Church (1896) designed by architect Edmond Jacques Eckel (1845–1934), United Presbyterian Church (1901), First Church of Christ Scientist (1905), First English Evangelical Lutheran Church (1913), and Queen of the Apostles Roman Catholic Church (1908) designed by Eckel. 

Boundary increased in 2009. 

Source: Wikipedia
10:
### Commerce and Banking Historic District (2001)
---  

A national historic district that encompasses 39 contributing buildings in the central business district of St. Joseph. <!--more--> Between 1859 and 1950, various architectural styles were developed, including Italianate, Classical Revival, and Streamline Moderne. 

Located in the district are the separately listed German-American Bank Building, Corby-Forsee Building, Missouri Theater, Missouri Theater Building, and Missouri Valley Trust Company Historic District. Other notable buildings include the Ballinger Building (1889), Commerce Building (1889, 1941), First National Bank of St. Joseph (1902, 1963), Lehman's, Plymouth Building (1908), and the United Building (1917-1918) by the architecture firm of Eckel & Aldrich.
12:
### Dewey Avenue–West Rosine Historic District (2002)
 ---  

A national historic district that encompasses 37 contributing buildings and 1 contributing site in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. <!--more--> It developed between about 1880 and 1930, and includes representative examples of Italianate, Second Empire, Queen Anne, Colonial Revival, Tudor Revival, and American Craftsman style architecture.

Notable buildings include the Marshall B. Stroud House (1890), John and Frank Whitman House (1890), Johanna Johnson House (1908), Frank Lacy House (1900), and a number of speculative houses built by Charles H. Nowland. 

Source: Wikipedia
19: ### Hall Street Historic District (1979) --- A nationally recognized local historic area listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1979. The neighborhood, sometimes called *Millionaire's Row*, was developed between 1870 and 1920 and reflects St. Joseph’s peak prosperity during the westward expansion era, when the city had the highest per-capita income in the U.S. The district spans 19.4 acres (7.9 ha), and initially featured 43 contributing buildings that provided exceptional examples of Gilded Age architecture. It originally included Italianate, Second Empire, Chateauesque, Richardsonian Romanesque, and Queen Anne style homes. Although several structures have been lost to fire and neglect, many of the beautiful surviving homes have been preserved or restored and now serve as bed-and-breakfasts, apartments, or private residences. More Info
18: ### Cathedral Hill Historic District (2000) --- A national historic district that encompasses 309 contributing buildings, one contributing site, and contributing structures in a predominantly residential section of St. Joseph. It developed between about 1860 and 1950, and includes representative examples of Greek Revival, Italianate, Queen Anne, Colonial Revival, and American Craftsman style architecture. Located in the district is the separately listed Virginia Flats apartments. Other notable buildings include the Nisen Stone House (c. 1885), Thomas Culligan House (c. 1872), A. D. Hudnutt House (1909), St. Joseph Cathedral (1877), James Wall House (c. 1880), Taylor Apartments (c. 1860), E. F. Weitheimer House (1888), Sarah and Ann Walsh Apartment House (1915), Henry Owen Stable (1898), George T. Hoagland Speculative House (1901), and James Hull House (1887). Source: Wikipedia More Info
